stimulus: Earlier studies conducted within individual European countries had identified consistent links between personality traits—measured along five broad dimensions—and the kinds of food flavors people prefer. To test whether these links extend beyond a single national context, Anika Müller and colleagues surveyed more than 40,000 people across forty-eight countries on six continents about their personality traits and their flavor preferences for European-style cuisines (e.g., savory, smoky, herbal, tangy, sweet). The cross-cultural data confirmed that the five-dimension personality framework captured participants’ preferences for these European-style cuisines well and revealed broadly similar patterns linking traits to flavor preferences from country to country. These results suggest that ______blank
stem: Which choice most logically completes the text?

answerOptions.content: cross-cultural differences in the connection between personality traits and flavor preferences are smaller than scholars had previously believed.

the five-dimension framework can likely be used to predict preferences for non-European cuisines based on personality traits even though those cuisines differ substantially in flavor from European-style cuisines.
people who have more familiarity with European-style cuisines are likely to express stronger preferences for those cuisines than people with less familiarity are.
across different cultural settings, people who share similar profiles of personality traits tend to prefer similar European-style cuisines.

rationale:
Choice D is the best answer because it most logically completes the cross-cultural study's discussion. The earlier within-country studies showed that personality traits predict flavor preferences for European-style cuisines. The new study extended this to many countries and confirmed that the same five-dimension framework captured preferences well and produced similar trait-to-preference patterns across cultures. It follows that people with similar personality profiles tend to prefer similar European-style cuisines regardless of cultural setting.

Choice A is incorrect because the text does not state what researchers previously believed about cross-cultural variation in the strength of this link; it presents the consistency as a finding, not as a comparison to prior assumptions. Choice B is incorrect because the study examined only European-style cuisines, so its findings cannot be extended to non-European cuisines that differ substantially in flavor. Choice C is incorrect because the text does not discuss familiarity or exposure; it only addresses personality traits and preferences.
